summary: I was a sophomore in high school when Pearl Harbor was attacked in December 1941. I remember sitting at my desk whittling a model airplane when the news came over. Like many of my friends, I thought the Japanese were nuts and that we'd whip them quickly, reflecting the racist attitudes we had at the time. That attack unified the country and made World War II a very popular war among young people like me.

I was born and raised in Madison, Wisconsin, specifically at 2220 Keyes Avenue where my family had lived since 1918. I went to grade school at Dudgeon and graduated from Madison West High School in 1944. Madison was a fairly homogeneous place back then; I don't recall seeing any African Americans growing up there. It was also home to my dad's career as a University of Wisconsin professor.

I was born at St. Mary's Hospital in Madison on December 8, 1926. It was the place where my life began and where my family was rooted in the community. My upbringing in Madison, starting from that hospital, shaped much of my early experiences and connection to the city.

My dad was a professor at the University of Wisconsin–Madison in Agricultural Engineering, specializing in erosion control. He graduated from the university in 1914, and my mother also graduated from there in 1916, which was unusual for women at the time. The university played a big role in my family's life, and I grew up around that academic environment.

My dad was very anti-New Deal and held isolationist views during the time leading up to World War II. Although I didn't pay much attention to political discussions at home, I understood that my father's opinions were quite firm against the New Deal policies. This shaped some of the household atmosphere during my youth in the late 1930s and early 1940s.

I vividly remember seeing pictures in Life magazine and newspapers in 1941 showing our war games in Georgia. The photos showed soldiers training with broomsticks as machine guns and jeeps marked as tanks, highlighting how poorly equipped we were at the time. Those images stuck with me and reflected the early unpreparedness of the U.S. military before entering World War II.

In the fall of 1941, before the U.S. entered the war, I saw pictures of military training exercises in Georgia. The troops there were using broomsticks to simulate machine guns and marking jeeps as tanks because we lacked proper equipment. This was a clear sign of how unprepared we were at the time, and it made a lasting impression on me.

There was a Japanese family that had a truck farm near the Nine Springs Sewer Plant in Madison, and at least one of their children was in my graduating class. Despite the war and the general anti-Japanese sentiment, I never witnessed any animosity toward those kids at school. My mother raised us to be non-prejudiced, and that family had been part of the community for years.

I grew up after the Vietnam War and observed how different the attitude was compared to World War II. During Vietnam, many young men tried to avoid military service, which was the opposite of my generation's eagerness to enlist. That contrast always struck me when reflecting on my own desire to serve during World War II.

My second older brother worked for Boeing during World War II, contributing to aircraft design. Even though he wanted to join the military and had flight training, Boeing kept him on because of his importance to their work. This was a common practice during the war to keep skilled workers in critical industries.

My older sister joined the Women's Army Corps and served in California for about a year during World War II. She was one of only two siblings besides me who served in the military during the war. Her service was part of the broader mobilization of women in the armed forces at that time.
